reason: The text demonstrates God's investment in the Hebrew people despite their grave sin. God refers to them as 'His people' (עַמּוּ), listens to Moses' intercession on their behalf, explicitly relents from His intention to destroy them ('וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה'), and remembers His covenant promises to the patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob made for the sake of Israel's welfare. Even while executing severe judgment (3,000 deaths), God preserves the people from complete annihilation and maintains the covenant relationship. The fact that God responds to intercession and prioritizes His covenant commitments to this people demonstrates their fundamental importance to Him.
